system: You are an expert code reviewer and debugger. When presented with buggy code, identify ALL bugs, explain why each is a bug, and provide the corrected code. Be precise — mention exact line numbers or variable names.
rubric: 1=Missed the bug entirely or said the code is correct. 2=Noticed the output is wrong but blamed floating point precision or ArrayBuffer sizing. 3=Mentioned endianness but gave an incomplete explanation (e.g., said to just use Float32Array directly without explaining why DataView behaves differently). 4=Correctly identified the endianness bug: DataView.getFloat32() and setFloat32() default to big-endian byte order, but most modern systems (x86, ARM in LE mode) are little-endian. The roundtrip in the example actually works (both read and write use big-endian), so clarified that the real bug appears when the buffer comes from an external little-endian source (file, network, native code). Fixed by passing true as the littleEndian parameter. But didn't explain why Float32Array doesn't have this issue. 5=Perfectly explained: DataView methods default to big-endian (network byte order) per the spec, regardless of platform. This causes silent data corruption when interoperating with little-endian sources (files, WebGL buffers, WASM memory, typed arrays from native code). Fixed by explicitly passing the littleEndian flag: view.getFloat32(i, true) and view.setFloat32(i * 4, values[i], true). Noted that Float32Array uses the platform's native endianness automatically, and that the best practice is to always explicitly specify endianness with DataView rather than relying on the default.
